title: Katju's Dishonest Opinion Strangles Justice!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source scans were consulted to repair OCR damage in the Tamil reading copy, including the omitted salutation, the interview and closing dates, malformed Tamil words, and an English capital `I` misread as the numeral `1`. These repairs are confined to this translation file; the frozen source chapter is unchanged. Political, judicial, and legal allegations remain attributed to Kalaignar or to the speakers he cites.
paragraphs: I have great respect and regard for the judiciary and judges. Judges must be like a balance that weighs evenly, setting aside likes and dislikes, desires and aversions, and standing in the middle to deliver judgments and opinions. They must preserve that impartiality both while in office and after retirement. Yet our country has recently seen a few forget this standard and issue opinions as their minds please, weakening the judiciary, democracy's vital and trusted pillar, as though boring holes into it.

Justice Markandey Katju became Chief Justice of the Madras High Court in November 2004, served for only a year, later served for a time as a Supreme Court judge, and retired. After retiring from the Supreme Court in September 2011, he remained silent until now. During the past two days, however, he has published statements that a judge should not descend from his dignified position to make, apparently under some indirect pressure. The dignity of his high office and his own past have thereby been called into question, and the matter has reached Parliament.

Is not one drop of poison enough to spoil a pot of milk? With the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa reaching its final stage and judgment approaching, it appears that these statements have been planned to praise her and exert pressure upon the outcome.

Nearly ten years after serving as Chief Justice of the Madras High Court in 2004-05, Katju wrote online about that period on his Facebook page. *The Hindu* published it prominently with his photograph. He praised Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, saying she never interfered with appointments to the Madras High Court or with the judiciary's honest and independent functioning; never recommended anyone for appointment as a judge; and respected judicial independence so greatly that he encountered no problem during his year as Chief Justice.

We have no objection to Justice Katju praising the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister. But he also made an unnecessary, unnamed criticism of the DMK. That made it unavoidable for us to examine his own history.

In an article in *The Times of India* dated 21 July 2014, Katju wrote that a person had been appointed an additional judge of the Madras High Court despite complaints against him; that Katju recommended action to the Supreme Court; but that a Tamil Nadu party allied with the Union government protected the judge with the help of three Supreme Court judges.

If this claim is true, why did Katju not disclose it while serving as High Court Chief Justice or Supreme Court judge? Why did he remain silent throughout the Congress government's tenure if, as he now alleges, Prime Minister Manmohan Singh and a Congress minister supported what happened? After retiring, did he stay silent because he wanted the chairmanship of the Press Council of India? Why has he suddenly felt compelled to complain now, ten years after the alleged 2004 event? Why has he not named the High Court judge he accuses? Only if the name is given can that judge explain the facts and respond.

It is said that Katju was close to Pandit Nehru's family, that his father was a Chief Minister, and that his grandfather served in Nehru's Union Cabinet. Coming from a Congress family tradition, he enjoyed office and privileges as a High Court judge, Chief Justice and Supreme Court judge while Congress governed at the Centre, and received the high office of Press Council Chairman after retirement. How, then, did this sudden compulsion arise to accuse the Congress government and its Prime Minister ten years later, after that government had fallen? Must I explain what kind of person accepts office under a government and attacks it only after it is gone?
Indeed, various criticisms have been directed against Katju himself. Do you know what present Finance Minister Arun Jaitley said about his contradictory conduct on 18 February 2013?

Katju had criticised State governments ruled by non-Congress parties while declining to say anything about Congress. Jaitley asked whether he was making those accusations as a form of "thanks" to the party that gave him a major post after retirement.

Katju spoke against the Bihar, Gujarat and West Bengal governments. He attacked non-Congress governments and Chief Ministers, accused then Bihar Chief Minister Nitish Kumar of buying the media, and said he was not prepared to believe the account that Narendra Modi had no part in the 2002 Gujarat riots.

Jaitley said that as Press Council Chairman, Katju had forgotten the duties of his office and entered political activity, and should resign immediately. He said Katju's statements, as a judge and afterwards, were not merely surprising but shocking; that dignified opinion appeared unrelated to him; that no serving or retired judge should behave in an overtly harsh, startling or power-seeking manner; that Katju distorted facts; and that if he wished to engage in politics, he should leave an office equivalent in standing to that of a judge. Jaitley concluded that Katju had failed every test by which a judge's fitness should be assessed.

Katju had also made disparaging generalisations about large sections of India's people, provoking strong opposition from many quarters. He then publicly apologised: "From time to time people have been bringing to my notice that the language I use may have been harsh. So if it has hurt some feelings, I thought it fit to apologise."

Do you know what the same Katju said about West Bengal Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee? On 23 May 2012 he praised her: "Integrity is becoming rare in public life in the country, but Mamata's integrity is of the highest order." Yet on 29 November 2012, he reversed himself and said: "Mamata Banerjee seems to have become intolerant and whimsical, and she is autocratic."
So let us wait and see how Katju, who praises Jayalalithaa today, changes his words a few months from now.
Do you know the "lofty opinion" this same Katju expressed about the same Chief Minister last year?

During a Press Council inquiry concerning a Tamil Nadu journalist on 5 April 2013, counsel for the Tamil Nadu government expressed regret. Katju, then Press Council Chairman, said the government must either resign or administer according to the Constitution by immediately arresting, charge-sheeting and suspending thirty police officers accused of high-handed action against the editor of a Tamil daily and his son for writing about illegal granite mining.

When counsel said he was sorry, Katju replied in the source-printed English: "Your sorry will not do." He said the Chief Minister should admit that she could not run the government according to the Constitution and resign. He referred to two Press Council inquiry orders dated 27 April and 27 August 2012 that the Tamil Nadu authorities had not obeyed, and said that if the State government could not administer according to the Constitution, it had no right to remain in office.
These were Katju's severe observations because the AIADMK government had not pursued action over an incident that occurred during the DMK government and had not complied with his orders.
The same Justice Katju who yesterday praised Jayalalithaa as someone who never interfered in the judiciary had angrily warned her only last year to leave office.

In an NDTV interview broadcast on 21 July 2014, Katju alleged that three former Chief Justices of India, R. C. Lahoti, K. G. Balakrishnan and Y. K. Sabharwal, had improperly compromised and yielded to political pressure. He alleged that Sabharwal extended the tenure of a judge facing corruption allegations and that Balakrishnan later made that judge's position permanent.

When NDTV correspondent Nidhi Razdan asked why he had waited so many years to publish such sensational allegations, Katju replied, "Only examine whether what I said is correct," and walked out halfway through the interview. Was it proper for the Chairman of the Press Council of India to lose patience with a media organisation in this manner?
I have explained what kind of judge has praised Jayalalithaa. Shall I briefly describe how the Chief Minister he praised behaved towards judges?
It was alleged that after a High Court judge delivered a ruling against Jayalalithaa, electricity and water connections to the judge's home were cut.
Under the AIADMK government, a judge's son-in-law was arrested on an allegation that he possessed cannabis.
It was alleged that a High Court judge who did not grant bail was removed from office after representations were made to those who mattered.
Jayalalithaa also argued all the way to the Supreme Court that a case against her should be heard only by the judge she specified.

Former Chief Justices Lahoti and K. G. Balakrishnan rejected Katju's allegations. R. C. Lahoti said: "Everything is documented. The records contain the reasons for what I did and did not do. I committed no wrong in my life."

K. G. Balakrishnan said Katju's allegation was baseless and untrue; that it was wrong to say the judicial appointment had been made under political pressure; and that it was unfair to make the accusation ten years later when the person accused was no longer alive. He said he did not know of the CBI report concerning the judge; that they acted because they considered transfer to the Andhra Pradesh High Court appropriate; and that there had been neither political pressure nor corruption behind it.

Katju served only one year as Chief Justice of the Madras High Court in 2004-05. What purpose lies behind his hymn of praise claiming that Jayalalithaa never interfered in the judiciary? Could one year as Chief Justice have given him complete knowledge of her? Did this same judge not call upon the same Jayalalithaa to resign only last year?

After serving many years as a judge and reaching the Supreme Court, to throw dust casually upon former Chief Justices many years later can only be described as an attempt to demean the judiciary itself.

An examination of Katju's past conduct shows that he speaks inconsistently; departs from judicial impartiality; loses patience while expressing opinions; becomes angry; may deny by evening what he said hastily in the morning; and is an embodiment of contradictions. Anyone can easily understand whom his latest statement is intended to help.
I do not regard being compelled to set out these truths about a former judge as a welcome opportunity.
With affection,
M. K.
23 July 2014
